# Voyager Mission Overview

The Voyager program sent two robotic probes to survey the outer planets of the solar system. Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 launched in 1977 from Cape Canaveral, taking advantage of a rare planetary alignment. Engineers designed the spacecraft to operate far longer than their original mission plan required. Both probes carried a suite of cameras, spectrometers, and particle detectors tuned for the cold outer solar system. Mission planners at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory coordinated a trajectory that used gravity assists at each planet to reach the next one faster. This technique, sometimes called the planetary grand tour, was only possible because of a alignment that occurs roughly once every 175 years.

The probes returned the first detailed images of Jupiter's turbulent atmosphere and its four largest moons. Scientists were surprised to find active volcanoes on Io, the innermost of the Galilean moons. Europa's icy shell showed long cracks that hinted at a liquid ocean underneath. These early discoveries reshaped how researchers thought about moons in the outer solar system. Before Voyager, most moons were assumed to be geologically dead, cratered relics unchanged for billions of years. The data suggested instead that tidal forces from Jupiter could keep a moon's interior warm enough for ongoing geological activity.

## Saturn and Beyond

After their encounters with Jupiter, both spacecraft continued on to Saturn, arriving with instruments recalibrated for the ringed planet's fainter light. Voyager 1's trajectory was bent by Saturn's gravity to send it out of the plane of the solar system entirely, ending its tour of the planets. Voyager 2, however, stayed close to the ecliptic plane and went on to visit Uranus and Neptune. It remains the only spacecraft to have flown past either ice giant. At Neptune, Voyager 2 discovered active geysers on the moon Triton, spewing nitrogen gas and dark material several kilometers into the thin atmosphere. Engineers on the ground had to send new flight software to the spacecraft to handle the dimmer sunlight so far from the sun.

Both probes are now traveling through interstellar space, beyond the heliopause where the solar wind gives way to the interstellar medium. Their radioisotope thermoelectric generators produce less power every year, and mission controllers have had to switch off instruments one by one to conserve energy. Despite the aging hardware, both spacecraft still return a small trickle of data about the particles and magnetic fields found this far from the sun. Communication takes over twenty hours one way at the current distance. The team maintaining the mission expects to keep at least one instrument running into the early 2030s if the power budget allows it.

## Legacy of the Mission

The Voyager Golden Record, a phonograph disc carrying sounds and images of Earth, rides aboard each spacecraft in case either is ever found by another civilization. It includes greetings in dozens of languages, recordings of natural sounds, and a curated selection of music. The record was assembled by a committee led by Carl Sagan in the months before launch. Decades later, it remains a symbol of the mission's ambition, reaching far beyond its original scientific goals. Many of the engineers who built the spacecraft have since retired, but new generations of scientists continue to analyze the data that keeps arriving.

The mission has directly influenced the design of nearly every outer planets mission that followed it, from Galileo at Jupiter to Cassini at Saturn. Its gravity-assist trajectory planning became a standard technique still used for missions headed to the far reaches of the solar system. Engineers studying long-duration spacecraft reliability still reference Voyager's redundant systems and conservative power margins. The probes were never expected to last this long, and their continued operation is often cited as one of the most successful engineering efforts in the history of spaceflight.
